words: Anyway, you know, I was sawing down the mill. Had Sawyer. They had four carriages, you know. Each one had a head Sawyer. You know. Yeah. Anyway, and she was teaching school up here at Chambers Flat. And I had about eight or nine head of cows, bunch of beef cattle to feed and take care of. And I was running a farm too. And so I had to have, you know, three gallons of gas a week, you know, as your allotment. Three gallon a week was running too. Well, I had that much on two cars. So I had that old Dodge, old twenty seven Dodge. We had that. And then I had this forty two Plymouth. And so she had to go to school and I had to go to the mill. And of course we had to take her cream to town and run around. And I went over there to the office in Moscow to try to get some extra gas. No. No. Just couldn't do it. Seems like everybody went there to get extra gas. They figured it was a damn liar, you know. He didn't need it, you know. Yeah. So I went over a couple times and they turned me down. No, I just can't give you any extra gas. I told them why I needed it for them. So, my gosh, she had to go to school. I had to go to work. I had to do my run around on the farm, you know, to take cream to town. And we were just going day and night, both of us. And so I just didn't change my speed around the cars. I could get gas to farm with. Barrel gas, but you wasn't supposed to use that on the road, you know. I could get all the gas I wanted for the barrel, but then I couldn't get the tickets to go car gas. So I just pumped gas into the car tax and kept on going.

words: Was that marked gas, the farm gas? Was the farm gas marked?

words: No, you had to rise them. But they'd go by your speedometer every month. You had to turn in your speedometer reading, you know. Well, I just turned in in order to get that three gallon a week. He says, how come, well, what's the beef? It seems like you said your car is making pretty good gas mileage. I said, no, no, I don't think it is. I said, I got two of mine. I don't think either one of them is making any extra ordinary gas mileage. Well, according to the speedometer reading, you are. There's an amount of gas you're supposed to have. I said, no. I said, no. I'm burning gas. Well, where are you getting the gas? I said, getting it out of the farm barrel. Oh, boy, he's right through me in jail right now. That old, that old, that old guy that used to run them cedars over there at Moscow. Kenworthy. Yeah, he was ahead of it. So I told him, well, he wanted to know what I was using the gas for. I said, well, my wife is driving about eight or ten miles a day. Going to school and back. I'm holding the job down in the mill. I'm milking so many cows. And I said, I'm doing some farm work, or farming. And then I says, because you can do that on three gallon of gas a week. And you come over and show me how. Well, he says, you said your wife teaches in school? I said, yeah. And I said, I'm holding the job down in the mill. I'm a farmer. I'm milking so many cows, chickens, pigs to feed. Well, he says, why didn't you say something about that? I says, Mr. I've been over here and cried my eyes out trying to get some extra gas legal. So, well, he says, you go home. You'll get some extra gas. So the next day, I got home the next day in the mail. There was a string of tickets. It was choking ox. Well, I couldn't start to use them all, you know. It's crazy. So, but I got you know, but if you take the extra tickets and turn them back, you didn't need them, well then they'd charge you that much. So I just...

And I didn't, you know, waste gas or do anything loose because I didn't have time to go out and enjoy a ride.

Well, it's all in a kind of breeze. Well, it's just like a railroad track. You know, going through the snow, your car kind of shifts a little bit once in a while. You couldn't steer it. Once you got started, the other side of the highway was in them ruts. I could go clear up and down that old road. You couldn't hold the snow. You'd have to follow them ruts. Just like a railroad track. And I'd go in there and pick her up. The guys had been over three foot of snow on the road. But it was packed down, you know. And all winter long, I went in and out. speaker: Anna Utt
words: It was just weekends. That I'd seen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Movie show in Palouse? It was movies then?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they had movies in Palouse. They had a theater down in Potlatch too. Yeah, they had quite a deal. People would go to a show on a Saturday night. On their way back there. Of course, you wouldn't know where the old road was now. It's still there, but it's not traveled much anymore. You know where Candy Ford is? Oh yeah. The highway comes around there now. Well, right there at Candy Ford, that hall. You'd come from Palouse, you'd turn right, left. And it goes around up over the hills. And then down comes outdoors by that lumber company. Where that lumber company would blow pot lights there. That's where the road would come out. It would come down over them hills. And we'd come up there one night and it was muddy and raining. A whole bunch that was in the car had that old beating in my dad's. And I had to cut out wide open. Better like a bull. Anyway, we got over there. We just had to pass that Candy Ford outfit up over the hill there. Well, the headlights were all up above. Somebody ahead. It was just ruts up through there, you know. Mud roads. And there was nothing but headlights. It was as far as you could see. And so, the car stuck up there. Nobody could get by him. Oh gosh, you know. I looked up on the bank fence along here. But there was a bank out there so far from the fence. I said, lookin' good. And I head up on that bank and I bounced for ditches. I would clear up that whole bunch. You know, in the ruts and in the ditches and up on the banks. That old car hurt like it. But I passed everything. And boy oh boy, everybody in the country, that powerful Billy. Boy, the Billys were much as the damn fool driving the thing. He was lucky. Yeah, he was lucky. I didn't upset the damn thing. Anyway, we had a lot of fun. Everybody in the car was yelling, you know. Hold on to her. And anyway, I bet there was 40, 50 cars in that string lined up there. And I was around the whole bunch. And I had a head of them kept a going. And boy, that old powerful Billy. That old powerful Billy had a 40 horsepower motor in it. But had exhaust pipe about that big around. And better like an old tractor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your parents, did they object at all to your going with such a wild guy?

speaker: Unknown
words: Oh boy.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, they weren't too much. But different Harvard people. See, I was raised up at Harvard.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, it was like Dogpatch. It was like old, what do they call them guys back there?

speaker: Unknown
words: Dogpatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a little Abner.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, it was a family was always fighting back there. McCallies and that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah, yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Harvard and Princeton was quite like the country back there. Bunch of old women would get along the side of the line and they'd look at you and nose you. Like you walked in alpha mars or something. Princeton, I didn't have much use for Harvard. I'd say either. So, went along that way for years. So when I started going around, oh boy, I don't know. And I used to, I could pay my ass for it. I used to go pay the old Billy and this bell would go up and you'd go up and you'd go up to the medical center and you'd leave a dust streak, you know, by the time you leave Princeton, you'd get up there and that old car bellied and you'd come around the corner and there'd be an old farmer out there next to the fence. Whoa boy, hold these horses.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Hey boy. Of course.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So you know what they call me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then when you started going there, they probably thought it was too bad for her.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, I suppose.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: My dad said, I asked him one time, he said, well, I suppose you'll have a better time, you know, have more fun if you go with a guy like him than somebody, you know. I don't even know who he compared him with now.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Probably some farmer boy.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But anyway, he was always was good to him. We had her come by down here and he'd come down and help him harvest and they were real good pals.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, he'd get some low sex for me. Yeah, we got along all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you do a lot of planning before you got married? Thinking about, you know, well, yeah, I mean getting married and what you would need to be married as far as, you know, setting up a house and all that stuff, thinking about the future. Did kids do that in those days?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, no, I just kind of went from day to day. Well, we decided, well, we got engaged and she had been teaching, but I think, you didn't have a job teaching at all yet, I don't think.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, but I had one more year before I'd have to go back to school and we talked about it. And then the mill shut down.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they had low water and they didn't have enough water to float the log down at the dam, you know, the logs laying on the ground. So they had to shut the mill down. So I told Anna, well, why don't we go get married and go on our honeymoon for all our mill shutdowns? So that's what we did.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then we come back and we went to Garfield and got our stove and...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Rented the house down potlatch.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Rented the house, well, after a while. We couldn't get one right off. I thought it was a few weeks, wasn't it, before we got one?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, I don't know. We had a boarder in for a house, finally got it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then we moved in with our bedroom set and our kitchen out there.

speaker: Unknown
words: I was trying to...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I got one of those shoes, they call it, they say. Got what we needed. You got a stove?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went to Garfield to get a stove?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, yeah, that's where we bought a stove. They had a hardware over there. Made my island run us a hardware store. So we went over there and then we bought some other furniture down at Gibby Williams' pluice.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then that spring, we bought a... No, you had a 22 and I got me one.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, we used to go squirrel shooting, so I got her a little 22.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Just a little one. But man, we were deaf on squirrels for a while. And then I just couldn't see them at all. I'd just miss every shot.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: She was a Deadeye. What do they call her? What was this Deadeye?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, that's not a... But anyway, I could shoot a wire. We were not.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know where a wire is stapled to a post? Yeah. She could split the wire. Not only once, she could do it three times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you use any of that food? Did you use any of that for food when you shot the animals? Or did you use the skins? Or just for play?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, just to get rid of them. Farmers were almost paying bounty on them to get rid of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Squirrels, sure.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You don't give much anymore now. They're pretty well done rid of them. Boy, when I was a kid around here, gee, Christ. Right up this road, there'd be squirrels running back and forth across the road in front of your car all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you get Shiv Reed after you got married? Yeah.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, we had... We had a shower up in Harvard and I had one down here to Princeton. And then we had a Shiv Reed. And it was just beautiful. Yeah. I got so many, many... well, two showers, you know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: It worked out all right, except one of your old boyfriends had the Shiv Reed. And I just had a box of cigars out, you know. There wasn't all that many people here. Somebody told me afterwards he went out with pockets full of cigars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he got something out of it anyway.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He got something out of it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, but that was...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: By the way, spell Shiv Reed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I thought there was two ways. The easy way I've seen it is S-H-I-V-A-R-E-E. But I've also seen it spelled C-H... Like Shaviary or something.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes, that's the way.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: It's French. I think it's spelled the French way. C-H-A-R-I-V-A-R-I. Yeah. It doesn't sound like Shaviary, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's pronounced Shaviary too?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I guess so. You look at my dictionary, Shaviary. And that's the way to spell it and that's what it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the showers like then, Anna? Did they give you gifts?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, gifts. Pillow slips and blankets. Or a lot of dishes. I got that covered back in there. Most of them. A lot of wedding gifts in there. I'll have to divide up one day.

speaker: Unknown
words: No.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They don't shiver you anymore. I haven't heard of shivery around here anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they surprise you? Was that part of it? I mean, did you know the one was coming?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Just wait till you get to bed, you know. That makes you get up and dress. I know one time I got shot one time with a shivery and my sister got mad. We was living on the other place up there. Sleeping up there, you know. So, I think, guys, when they started to come in, they were shooting shotguns off and beating on cans, you know. And we had an old shepherd dog. And I never seen a dog as brave as he was, but... He went out and met the whole bunch of them. They were banging away and raising their neck, you know. And he went out to any man who was teaching school with a prince named Alfred Chaba. But I guess he bit him.

speaker: Unknown
words: And...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Anyway, they come on up to the house. And, of course... That's...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: What kind of work is that?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That's what they call a carnival.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it's lovely.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Carnival glass, I think.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then I got two or three like this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, that's nice. Those are shower gifts? Oh, really nice.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then the old stacks of glasses, you know. Fruit bowls and little sauce dishes and that kind of stuff.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I wonder why they call that carnival glass. Is that what they used to give to prizes at carnivals?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it looks too nice for that. Maybe it's the colors, because it has the... It reflects so many different colors in it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It has lots of colors in it. Um... Carrie. Reynolds has one almost exactly like this. Only it's primary doesn't look... Ruffles in it, so much. It looks like the same color and everything.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, that's carnival glass. That was depression glass. You see that at advertising. These guys are telling me it's depression glass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were telling me a story about the dog bit...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah, I bit that guy. But anyway, they come up on the house while all the rockets woke me up, see. I was just a little kid. And so I went and looked out the window. I think it was summertime, and I think they had the window raised up so far. And I went and stuck my head out. And as I stuck my head out, I seen a ball of fire right down below me. And so some guy just up and pulled his shotgun off, you know, in the air. And he didn't have it straight up. So the next day I went out the house, right up just above the window. There was an area on the roof, you know. Birds out all around the place. So the little house fell down now. It was still there. That old shot didn't miss my aim. Not going to very far. He's just out there by the house, he's just bagging, you know, just like that. And I had my head stuck out there. I looked down there. Boy, I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first start working in the mill? That was before you two were married, right?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. I started working down in the mill during World War I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That early?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: About 1918.

speaker: Unknown
words: Well,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: only after the United States got into it. well, I don't know, all the kids was able to work and get a job because they pulled so many men out, you know, and go to war. They put boys and women to work and that's when I started working. Picking engines down in the mill. I think I was about 17, 18.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gee, if that now, if that, because weren't you born in 1903? You must have been even younger. Let me see. If it was 18 and you was born in three,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: you would have been 15.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think we got in at 17. 17. So you must have been about 14, 15.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I wasn't that young. There's something wrong with my story there. It must have been right after the World War. It seemed like I was 17, 18 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they still have a 10 hour day when you first started working there?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, no, eight hours.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, they...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I think about 1914 it would have lasted a 10 hour day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the working conditions like when you went into the mill? I guess the Wobblies had already cleaned it up as far as the 10 hour day goes. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: The Wobblies had already done their good work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the work like? Did you get many breaks during the day or anything like that?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, the work wasn't too hard. They always had a lot of men. You know, that's the point they tried to double the work up on guys like they do now, you know. No, they just... Kids go down there and like pick an edge and you just set to the right guys and pick the edges off as they go by off the boards. And it wasn't no hard work. The pay wasn't so hot. It was three dollars and forty cents a day.

speaker: Unknown
words: But...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I had a lot of money for a kid that had never had any money though. No, it wasn't six days a week. No, when they first started the mill up down here, they started up two shifts, 12 hour days. Yeah, 12 hour days. Kept the old mill going day and night. But I don't know, that run, I started up about 1906 I think when they got there. They got the mill right in there someplace. I think it was about 1914. Well, they finally got down to 10 hours and then finally down to 8. I think about 1914 was the last of the 10 hour days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when I've been talking to people in Potlatch, I've heard quite a bit about all the foreigners that worked there. Oh, yeah. Did you know or see much of the Greeks and Italians and Japanese in there?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, I seen that mill. Well, that was during the... Let me see, that would be World War I. World War II. It wasn't over six men that saw me then. It was white. It was all Greeks, Italians, Japs. They had Japs.

speaker: Unknown
words: Italians. Greeks.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they had four carriages down there, you know. And they had two men on a carriage. Long time ago, I was the only one that was... I was a whopper of Greek. All the rest of them. But then, during the Depression, is when they got thinned out a lot. A lot of them took off during the Depression and never come back.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Did... Were they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they stick to themselves pretty much? Yeah. The Greeks or the Italians?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, they used to be in the middle of the supper time, you know. They'd all get around a bunch, them Greeks. And there was Alec McDowell and I. There was only about three or four guys in there, outside of the Sawyers. That one Greek.

speaker: Unknown
words: And...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And guys, they'd get to yakking like a bunch of chickens over there. They'd walk me down and say, hey, you sons of beasts! I'd just get talking and go back to Greece. Oh, they used to give them a hard time. Oh, yeah, they'd congregate by themselves. They'd associate. Well, I guess you couldn't boil them. But anyway... Well, there was only about... One Greek left in Botlage, I guess. I know of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gus Demus?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, old Gus. I guess he was the only one left. Yeah. That's true. Well, I guess old Gus was the last one in the bunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they, that the foreigners were sort of discriminated against so far as getting the better jobs? Do you think they had a harder time than the Americans?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I think they were favored. Well, there was a reason for it. I don't know if... I can't prove when I say that, but... They'd do anything for a job. And ordinary guys out here, none of them ever married. You know, they was all single bucks and they'd all back together and it didn't cost them much to live. But they'd kick back to their boss, see. That's what I've heard now. I've never seen that, but I think there must be something to it. Because they'd kick back so much a month to their boss, and the boss would work them up in the job. So they didn't have much trouble working up and getting the better jobs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder how come the Greeks were in the sawmill and the Italians were in the yards? I mean, why they worked out that way? Did you ever think about, figure out why that was?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I guess it wasn't very, very hard. The Italians were in the sawmill, but there was a lot of them out in the planer. They worked on the... That's when they had little railroads running all over the yard out there. They had all the lumber around them. They had a lot of them working out there on the sections and building tracks. And then there was some... quite a few of them worked on the green chain.

speaker: Unknown
words: But...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know why that was. The Italians didn't mix very good. Well, I guess they don't to this day, do they? I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're both Southern Europeans, but that doesn't mean much. That doesn't mean you... I don't really know. Did you have any make friends with any of the Greeks in the sawmill? Oh, yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: After the World War II, there was old Gus and John Gaynor.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: John Gaynor was a good friend of his.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, him and I were good friends.

speaker: Unknown
words: Gus...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I knew him pretty good, but I didn't know him because I did John. But then there was John's brother, Tony.

speaker: Unknown
words: And...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they were all good guys.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they were.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Got along with them all right. But the rest of them... They just disappeared. I don't know where they went to. One of them, I don't know. He, like I say, he squeezed his money. And when the depression come... His name, I don't know what his name was. They called him Sleepy Jim. That's all I ever knew about him, Sleepy Jim. And he was kind of a laggard guy. He walked with a, you know, like he was all disjointed. Well, he saved his money. So I guess when depression come, he pulled out. Some of the other Greeks told me, I guess him and another guy went together and they bought a big hotel down in Seattle. And then they made money on that, my guys, and then bought another one. And I guess they got to be millionaires, both of them two Greeks.

speaker: Unknown
words: Yeah. Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Japanese? They were pretty much separate from everybody else too, weren't they? As far as the Japanese, yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh yeah, they had their own boarding house. Company built a boarding house just for the Japs down there. Yeah, they kept themselves pretty well. Well, there's one little Jap, used to janitor in the store and down at the main office. Wapenabi, I think is his name, something like that. Just a little fella. He had a little wife and two or three little kids. But then when Pearl Harbor hit, I guess they just railroad them out to a concentration camp, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I heard that there was talk that he was an agent of some kind and maybe that's what they thought about all the Japanese then, I don't know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well anyway, this guy, he was just a janitor, but yet he had a really expensive camera. And he'd talk to different guys to take him around the country, you know, they'd go to Lewiston and he'd take him pictures. And nobody thought nothing about it, you know, until after it was over with. And they wondered how he could afford such a camera he had. Well, that was really some camera. And of course, Pearl Harbor, that's why they figured he was just working as a spy and suddenly taking pictures and sending them all the mail down here to Lewiston, I suppose this one, and bams or whatever they were, they'd think he'd get around. He'd pay guys for taking him. You know, a man just working for that kind of job them days didn't make that kind of money. So anyway, everybody just goes along with it until Pearl Harbor broke loose and then all of a sudden, they was ready to take him out and get him alive. He was a good little guy up until that point. But I got to know him and he disappeared out. I was reading the other day about, a lot of them were good citizens. Took them just like, hauled them off like cattle in trucks, you know, and sent them in them concentration camps. And it wasn't fair.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But didn't a lot of them know about Pearl Harbor before it happened and get out of here?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, yeah, they started before Pearl Harbor, a lot of them started to disappear out of the potlice. I don't know where they went. Oh, there was one little Jap Edgeman daughter. I worked with him. Oh, way back in the early 20s. Way back in the early 20s. He kept on saying, someday Jap Engle will win this country. I'd say, oh, they couldn't lick one side of it. Oh, yes they will. Someday Jap Engle will win this country. I guess you know, I believe they was planning that a long time before Pearl Harbor. You'd say, someday Jap Engle will win this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe he could just see that it was coming because of the history of the thing. I mean, they got a different way of looking at history over there in the east, in the Orient than we do here.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I think the country must have been planning, you know, just waiting until they got ready to go. Probably that might have been 10 or 15 years they headed to Pearl Harbor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, I would have thought that those foreign groups now, the idea I've got from Gus Demas, I talked to him some, is that these Greeks and Italians didn't speak much English and neither did the Japanese. I think that would be pretty hard on groups like that to be a part of the community if they couldn't speak an English language.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, most of them tried to speak a little bit. Old John Gaynor and Tony, they, he had to be used to them to understand them. But they got so they could talk pretty good. But anyway, they, no, they weren't bad guys, but most of them wasn't even citizens. I don't know. Gus would tell me one time he went back to Greece, oh, six years ago, seven years ago, maybe more than that time goes. But anyway, he took an extra pair of shoes with him, you know, and his cousin was over there. He used to be at the mill, but he went back to Greece and got married. So Gus was over there. And by Gus, the people were so poor, you know, they just almost fought over that extra pair of shoes old Gus had. So finally he gives a pair of shoes to his cousin, and Gus didn't stay long. I guess he'd give a lot of money to his mother. He was pretty well healed one time, saved his money all his life. But I guess he'd give a lot of his money away over there. He kept enough of his figures to live the rest of his life over here. He gave a lot of his clothes away, took clothes over there. People just so hard up over there, you just couldn't say no to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He mentioned to me that his sister wasn't in very good shape over there. I wouldn't be surprised if he didn't help her out quite a bit.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I imagine, yeah. But he didn't like it over there. He went over there to stay, I guess. After he was there, he bought him a ticket back to the USA.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when those guys, the Greeks, I heard the Greeks did quite a bit of gambling, and the Italians did quite a bit of bootlegging in here, bootlegging wine anyway.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, the Greeks weren't as bad as the Dagos. There used to be an old dangle Mike they called in that honor way. Mike Petrogala was his name. He was a bootlegger from way back. You probably heard him say, when you buy a model of these boards, you can have any color you want. One of them was black. Well, that was the truth. That was the only color. An old Mike, everyone knew how he'd get a new board. But before he'd take it on the road, he'd take it in, paint or yellow. Paint or yellow before he'd ever take it out on the road. They'd always tell him to take a Mike car when he got down the road, because it was real yellow. I don't know why, but he'd take it in, paint or yellow before it ever went on the road. I don't know why, but he'd take it in, paint or yellow before it ever went on the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, do you think that there was much I've heard about that tricks and jokes were a big thing in the mill. It was a way, you know, way past the time, I guess. Do you remember there being very much of that when you were in the mill, jobbing other guys and playing practical jokes? Well, one guy told me that he remembers a favorite one was sticking grease in a guy's glove.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know about that. The only thing that was kind of fun one night down there, they, well, I discovered the fire, but I was riding number one carriage. You look up on the carriage, you can look out the window, and they had a conveyor run out there. The fire hose carried the sawdust out to the fire engine, and they had a fire hose. I looked down there, and I'd seen a fire burning. So, either they'd yell out at me, and I jumped off the carriage, and they had a fire hose up there, and so it had to run like this at the edge of the mill here. The fire hose was here. When they turned it on, it rolled up on the ridge here. But you had to run it down here, and then poke it out the window. Down on the fire. So, I was going to be the engineer to turn the water on. I don't know George. George Turner. He was one of them, but they went down there, you know, they'd run that thing down, and pull it around, and haul the turn around. Well, to turn around easy, I just gave it a whin. Of course, that hose just straightened out. Some guys went backwards over the tracks there on the carriage. I was down. And they always would have done that on purpose. Well, they always turned around, you know, but you know, I'd have been holding it straight. They had that thing that fell out the window. Of course, that old person hit that hose, and she just straightened out, and stuck them guys with them. They always blamed me for doing that on purpose. You didn't do it on purpose. I'd have turned around, so I turned around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would a fire like that get going?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know how to try that fire. It could be friction. Maybe a bearing got out of that chain or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the fire hole? You said they got a fire hole there?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, a fire hole, that's where they got the fires, to heat make the steam.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean the boiler rooms. Yeah, the boiler rooms.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Down there where they'd watch the fires, and boilers up above. Down there where they'd have to keep sawdust in the fire down there. They'd have to keep drilling all them boilers. I don't know why they called it the fire hole. They always called it. No, them guys, I didn't do it on purpose. They run the hose down there, and hope that it went and turn around. It'll turn around like I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you going down there from Princeton every day to work in the mill in those days? In the early years you worked there?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: First I got out, another kid and I, we stayed at the boarding house down at Potlatch. Then we come home on weekends. We both had bicycles. Then we got tired of that. The boarding house was grubbed in. I hadn't played it out on you after a while. No, a long time I lived up here on the old place and working night shift. I'd ride my bike to the mill and back every day. Nights, no headlights on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You motorcycled. A motorcycle, not a bicycle.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, a bicycle. I didn't get the motorcycle until I was 23 years old I guess. Before I got a motorbike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a real difference between the local kids who came from the farming country around here, or the backwoods country and the people who were town people that lived in Potlatch?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, there was a lot of friction. Town people, oh yeah, farm kids, you know, the backwoods. Yeah, there was a lot of friction between town people.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes, it was awful hard down in Potlatch going to high school. You know, the kids were always in the backwoods. The town kids just about run that country kid out. Did one, I have a friend that they used to, she had to ride a horse to school way out in the country, you know, in order to come to school. And they'd do like that, hold her nose, because they said she smelled like a horse. She smelled like a horse. She took it until she couldn't take it, and then she quit. But I had a better deal than they did most because I had to work in Potlatch for my board, and then I would go to school. And so after a while, I got considered a town kid.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Why did you live with the better people?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, you know, I don't know if they were better people, but they sure thought they were.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They thought they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were going to high school, you were working for your board at the same time? Were you working at the house you lived at?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, you know, I worked with the people you know.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: The thing you do then was to have a little servant girl. And you go in and get a family to live with, and they'd feed you for doing the ironing and the dishwashing and taking care of the kids and stuff like that in the evenings and mornings so they could go to their parties and whatnot. I worked one year and a half for the store manager down there, and then I worked another year for...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That's as far as he made it. Him and I was down in the bottom basement of that old pool hall. It was his idea. He really invented that old ice cream on a stick and then truckled around the outdoors. But he didn't know exactly the formula. He had to experiment, so I helped him out of it. And of course, every stick wouldn't work where I could feed it. I put it in the pot of the ice cream. The way they do it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That sounds like a pretty good job.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, it wasn't no good for nothing else. I don't know, we worked down there a big issue every night. Trying to get the temperature just right so you get that ice cream just cold, you know, and you dip it in this hot chocolate and pull it out. And that would leave a coating of chocolate on it. But if you didn't have the chocolate hot enough, that ice cream wasn't cold enough. Why, you know, it just wouldn't work. So you had your thermometer there, you'd get that chocolate just boiling hot, you know. And you'd get that ice cream on a stick and shove it down that chocolate and pull it up. You could just take them and just cut them in there and just make them just give them nothing to it, you know. But I don't know how many chocolate he'd put down that didn't work. He didn't have the chocolate hot enough or the ice cream wasn't cold enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he just sell them at his place or did he sell them around the country?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: When he got them, he started selling them right down his own joint down there.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well then he shipped them out to other towns.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, he got the patent on them I think and then they went out. But I don't know, when I went to Portland I went in the joint down there in Portland, you know, and asked for ice cream and ice cream, and they looked at me like I had horns on. They didn't know what they were talking about.

speaker: Unknown
words: Anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anna, was that, did a number of kids do that? Come in to live in Potlatch to go to high school? Or were you special? Who you wanted to know if you did that?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, no, anybody could come that could stand it, you know. It didn't make any difference how they got there. When the year I graduated there was only 14 or 15. 15 kids I guess graduated that year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 15 kids from Potlatch and?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: In all the country around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean did the kids from Harvard, if you wanted to go to high school in Potlatch would you pretty much have to live in the town if you came from so far away as Harvard? Some kids came in every day so far?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: One thing about Potlatch, they run the school, they run everything. They hired the teachers of Potlatch Limber Company. They hired the teachers and they built a schoolhouse, they kept the schoolhouse up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they let you go without paying.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: The district we come from had to pay something for us.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, they did? For high school?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But probably the family didn't pay. Did your family think it would be easier for you to go to school by living in Potlatch? Was that the idea?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes, they did because we had no way of getting there. Well I had been working in Potlatch anyway in the summertime. So I wasn't new at that job. And then when I went to high school, why?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That's about the only way a country kid could go to school. They had to go to town and live.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: If you lived two or three miles, you could ride a horse. But living up at Harwood, I'd have to go down there and stay.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: When they moved over to Princeton, she'd walk the school back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, when your family moved to Princeton, you just walked to Potlatch?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: My family moved down to Princeton, and so we started walking. Most of the time we walked the railroad track.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard on you when you were a kid to be working in the well-to-do people's houses? Did you feel that was a hardship then?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes, it was harder working for my board lot than it was walking. Because you had so much work to do for the family in order to earn your keep.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they'd treat kids like that. Not all families, but some of them. Just like the white men used to do the Negroes, you know. You were just a servant. That's what you were, just a servant. How was that aunt of mine done to you when she met a dentist down at Potlatch? For her pay, she'd give you some old dress or something?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, she said that if I'd come and work for her, she'd give me a dollar a week, you know, besides my board. So I changed. I was staying in a good place, but a person needs money too. So I went to work for her. And I don't think I ever collected the dollar. She would give me some old block she'd worn out, you know, and she'd say, well, you take this for your pay and, let's see, it's about worth $6. And so that'd be six weeks I'd work for that thing. Probably I would never have it on. But, you know, that's the way it was. And I was glad to walk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kids, I think kids can be used pretty easily that way.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Of course, I knew what work was. My folks had a big family and I was the oldest girl, so I knew what it was to take care of kids. But the thing I didn't like most of all was ironing. And I did, I had, I ironed every night, you know, for...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Did they have electric iron at that time?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I suppose, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe, I don't know, was it electric or did she just heat it up on a stove or something?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It must have been electric. But she had two little kids and she would dress them in the morning and then at noon she would change their clothes and they were ready for her until her daddy come home. So that was two sets of clothes every day besides his white shirts he wore down to the store. And I just, I was so weary the time I'd get ready for bed. And I got up in the morning, got the kids up and gave them their breakfast.

speaker: Unknown
words: And...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: For almost for their boy dinner, but you didn't eat enough to... Well... Like a hank.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Not there I didn't.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh boy, no, that was two days.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But at Elsie's I got more than I wanted, more than enough.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, that's what I'm saying, it just depends on who you work for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think these people who were living on Nob Hill, I've heard about them that quite a few of them lived above their means, really. Oh boy.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, kind of a funny thing, during the war, World War II, there was a family living up there in Nob Hill and he was one of the foremen down at the mill. And they were living high. But anyway, down at the garage, that potlatch, the name was Tableton, they were running the garage. But all of a sudden the government pulled out, I guess, that anybody that didn't pay that much bill, a cash bill, you know. But for that month, well, you couldn't get no more credit. Well, I didn't know nothing about it, but it just happened I made a little money and I went in and paid my bill up there. And so that law went into effect. And so I know I came up to the garage to get some gas because I had to drive, when I lived on the other place up here, driving back and forth to work. And this high-gluten lady, she drove in there and told her to fill her tank. And so, I guess, she drove in and so they wanted her money and she said to charge it. And he went back and looked on his, they had pages and pages of names there, you know, in Els. And she said, if your name was on that list, you didn't get no gas. So anyway, I'm looking over and she says, you'll have to pay for it, but she didn't have the money to pay for it. So they drained the gas out of her tank. And of course, I, you know, they just drained the gas back out, that's all they could do. The law said they had to have their money. And so I pulled right behind her, and I was finding out it got hurt and shoved out of there. I pulled up and told the feller up. And the guy looked at me and went back in and I went in with him. I guess I shouldn't have been, but he was going down the list of names there, you know. And I didn't have my name on it, because like I said, I just got hit her lucky and wasn't paying my bill up. And so I guess my name wasn't on it. I act like it almost hurt his feelings. That kind of a thing. He was so sure, you know, he was going to tell me to go, you know. Just got out of there. But I guess my name wasn't on the list, and I believe it really hurt his feelings, but I got you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, those people that you worked for that didn't care how much you had to eat really, they just used you like a servant, pretty much?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It didn't matter, you know, you had it to do. But the first people I stayed with, I was invited to the table to eat, you know, with the family. But the second time that I wasn't, I had to eat in the kitchen. I had to serve then and go back in the kitchen and eat what I could. And I don't know, now that I'm old, I don't care much for them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know what?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I don't care too much for them. But she was pretty good to brag on me. Well, my girl does this and my girl does that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: My girl?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But I got in trouble more times there because...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Who was that, Anna?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Walters.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, Walters, Anna.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: He ran the store down there, and he would bring, you know, candy and stuff home. But, you know, I knew better than to touch it. But I had company, my girlfriend from up at Harvard stopped in to see me. And she saw candy there, and when she saw candy, she just tasted it, you know. And she'd eat and eat and eat, and I didn't know how to stop her. I was too dumb to say, well, that's not for me. And holy cow, next morning did I get it. Hey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They just don't sound like nice people as far as...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, they wouldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems like they shouldn't have understood that you were as good as they were, even if you were working for them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, there was a whole lot like that, too, that turned about the old manager at Laird. I put off the road down here. He was so important that us Hicks put to get off the road for him. Until I taught him a lesson. Then he found out my girlfriend wasn't paying off. But then that was the idea. You know, he'd sit there, you couldn't say he fired a chauffeur when he got in trouble. But yet, you couldn't say he was riding down the street and everybody would get thrown off the road so he goes through. And he wasn't enjoying that. He was enjoying that. Watching all the Hicks get off the road for him.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But it was nice to be ratted on, you know. It was nice to have the ladies brag on you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She bragged on you to other ladies? To her friends?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: You know, my girl does this and that. I told my girl to do this and that, you know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they had a two-class society. Now they'd have dancers down in that old gymnasium. And just to dance for the big shots. I or her, but we couldn't go there, you know. We were outcasts. And of course we'd go in and get up on the balcony and look down and watch the High Buckle Bucks dance dance. Anyway, I don't know. Some guy got a whole bunch of thick bobs. Someplace. I was up there and I didn't know it. But I guess he threw them down the dance floor, you know. It didn't take long to break up the dance. Over there, he was so furious. Oh, if you could have found out who'd done that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I bet.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You never found out. But that was the last dance I had of that High Buckle Bucks dance. Two-class. That was the last time they ever had a dance. That broke it up. No more of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the houses on Nob Hill have servants? Have a girl working for them? Was that the common way to do it?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, I don't think they would.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Only the most well-to-do?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Anybody they could get used to work for them for nothing. And if they could keep you, well, that was a big thing. But, uh... When I stayed with old Dr. Thompson's, and Mrs. Thompson was a jewel. Huh. She was awful tight. She'd be at the table and she'd look all over. Do you want a slice of... No, she said, What do you want? If I said bread or something like that. Whole slice or half a slice? Of course, I'd always say a half a slice. But that, if I'd have said a whole slice, she would have... She was generous and all that, but she wasn't one to waste anything. As soon as I understood that, I just, uh... And she was very careful to see that I got enough sleep. And, um... She was the one that taught me the most about how to get along with those kind of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was one of them herself.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Mm-hmm. Yeah, she was one. And she was... She was... a gracious lady or whatever you call it. She truly was. And then, her kids were bigger than general babies, too. Mm-hmm. And so they didn't need any babysitting with them. I just, uh... The kids would ask, can I do this and that or the other thing? And I'd have to decide whether they could or they couldn't if they hadn't asked her ahead of time. But that was a real nice place to stay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She taught you how to get along. Was that like, um... Were there really certain things you were supposed to do or not supposed to do when you were dealing with those people? Yeah. I mean, certain ways of acting?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Uh-huh. And she told me... Well, for instance, one thing, it just doesn't mean anything, but it's one thing I remember especially. I always wash my hands before I go to do anything. Well, this time, I had the soap in my hand and I was just really washing. And she says, well, dear, I would just take a little soap and then wash my hands with water. You know, I just wouldn't wash the soap. Well, that's really what I was doing. And so, that was one way she taught me. And just little suggestions in that way with anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were living with those with the store people, were you supposed to not speak to them until you were spoken to and that kind of thing? Or did they have rules like that? I don't really know how people are supposed to be when they're supposed to be waiting on other people.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I don't know. You just...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, and you don't interrupt any conversation or, you know, just keep scary.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Kind of an irony that they happened, you know. Like I say, my aunt, she made a dentist. And, of course, I had to work for her. And, well, I guess, you know, she was just lowbrow, you know, just nobody, you know, just a servant. Well, it turned out I started going with that and married her, see. Well, I had not only worked for her, but she knew a lot of the going on around the house, you know. I got my ass cold.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: That woman would scream to the top of her voice. She'd scream and scream and scream. And she was... But then long, long afterward I saw her and Potlatch had advanced down there and she had gone down to change her shoes and we had to do a more comfortable pair of shoes. And she said, How are you, Anna? She said, I'm extremely glad you got into the family.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: She was a big guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm pretty happy about it too, except for you, lady. Yes.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But she knew.

speaker: Unknown
words: Yes.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yes, that got her going. I got married by a guy, you know. She knew Anna knew going along around that house with her.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: She wanted to get up in the morning, you know. She'd leave me a note here, leave me a note there, and if I'd miss a note, holy... Mmm, things would erupt. One time she left a note on the kitchen stove. I guess it was a kettle sitting on it or something. Anyway, I looked around and I didn't see the note. And what she wanted me to do was put a boiler on the stove and fill it full of water so the laundry maid could come in later and do the washing. And I didn't see that and I didn't put the water on the stove, of course. And I just did what I had to do otherwise. And pretty soon the laundry woman come and no water on the stove, no hot water to start with. Oh, man, Pearl was mad. Because she was wanting to get that woman out of there, you know, before noon. But if she left her notes in the same place so I could find them. Sometimes you could go in the living room and if she happened to be there the night before when she thought of what she wanted me to do, she'd write the note and leave it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just inconsiderate.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, she wasn't... You know, she wasn't a rich girl herself. She was just raised on a farm down here, you know. You know, families had a habit of doing that. Maybe they'd have a half a dozen kids or more. But they'd pick one kid out and they'd lavish everything. Well, it happened to be that family. It was the older kids. They had to work and do without and everything else. But the last two of them, I worked with my granddad down there in the Hayen, you know. And my uncle, he was what, three years older than I was. But his mother wouldn't let him work on that. I went out there and worked in Hayfield. There wasn't good enough work for him. I could go out there and work all night. But he couldn't. The most he could do was take the cars around town and get closer and stuff and all that. And my aunt, his sister, she just sat around and looked pretty. My mother would go down and have my mother in the kitchen getting dinner ready for the men, you know, cooking. And my daughter, she sat out in the front porch bag and looked pretty. She couldn't get her hands filed. Well, that's the way they raised her, see.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: She is the greatest, lovely daughter that ever was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she got herself a dentist, right? She went into the...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes, he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that shot her into the upper crust.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And she never let anybody forget it. But then they... One more day, they did a divorce. She was suing for divorce. And he died. And then... she married somebody, but I forgot who else.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You've been around probably a lot more than I have, but you know, I like how I worked on construction in Portland. You know, real millionaire, real rich man. Harry's wife. Don't make a difference. You know, you'd never know it. You'd be around. I didn't know how to work down there. But I was working on a man named Bowles. He made his money in World War I in the trip yard down there on the coast. Bowles. He built a big, well, a $300,000 house up on the console crest of Portland. And I worked on that job there. Well, he'd come around, you know, his old sloshing hat on, standing around. I thought he was just a sidewalk bum, you know. He'd stop you, you know, ask you about something, talk to you. Maybe talk to you. I wondered why I was talking to him when the boss didn't come around. You know, he'd keep holding you up and say, talking to you? Why are you supposed to be working? I couldn't figure that out. The boss didn't come around and tell me to lay off that talking business and get to work. Later I found out I was talking to this guy who was building a house, you know. And his wife says, the only thing is they'd never drive the same car. He had one of them old Franklin cars. They were cool Franklin's. And he had one of them that wasn't a new one. It was an old one. His daughter, when she'd come around, she'd drive the fastest car. But the old lady had an old car. She'd drive her out. She was friendly enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's a difference between the old rich and the new rich? Or the ones that are trying to get rich?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they're just two-bit millionaires. There's the worst kind of millionaires there is. I'm two-bit, but, you know, somebody gets a few dollars more than the other guy. And everybody's supposed to sleet them, flute them, and they go down the road or something. I always call them two-bit millionaires. But you take a real millionaire, a rich man, and, uh, I guess you know you can be around them. You know they don't allow the guy. They just, normal people.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: They don't brag. They can talk with a common man without trying to put him down, you know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I got to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know if it's still that way, but maybe, but probably in the old days it was. Well,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: today it's not so bad. I don't think it used to be. All that class distinction stuff isn't as bad today as it used to be. But I got a friend, I went to another kid that was running around here at home. His father died when he was small, and he had to go to school and work for money and have his mother and his sister live there. He just didn't have nothing. They just don't buy any poor is what he was in his poor. And then, anyway, he, I don't know, he got a hold of an old bicycle someplace and the front wheel went out. My mother sat in Montgomery Wharton at the Spokes and stepped to build a new wheel. And I helped him build that wheel up. Anyway, they were just so poor and pitiful. But he got tired out and he always made a reminder he was going to sell vacuum cleaners. Well, he was a backwoods boy and he couldn't talk, you know, strangers. He was the last guy in the world that would go door to door and sell vacuum cleaners. Well, he said it was nothing ever harder in the world and he put in the first day to sell vacuum cleaners. And the second day he said he just gave up. Some woman let him in and bought a vacuum cleaner for him. But otherwise they'd just go door to door and see him in the vacuum cleaner's light and the door to his face, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was he selling? Locally, around here?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Pocan. That's where he started out, Pocan. And, but finally he made a sale. So by gosh, he was all gung ho after that, you know. He was really well after that. And today he's still in vacuum cleaner business. Now that was when he was a kid. That's when he first started out and he started up in Pocan. Now he was head of offices in Portland. And maybe you've seen the papers talking vacuum cleaners.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: He's got stores all over the country, though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He owns it now? Now it's his company?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, he's retired now. But he's still got the, he's still owned, he's a general manager of all the stores. He's retired, but the company he owns fursuits him of Cadillac every year, brand new Cadillac every year. I guess they do yet, I don't know what they was up to a couple of years ago. Every year he'd get a brand new Cadillac to drive and he had his credit cards on the company, anything he wanted to buy. And then he had his salary so much a year from the company. But he was a millionaire time and again. All the thousands selling that first vacuum cleaner.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then during the war, everybody was selling everything they'd get to get little money and he went around collecting vacuum cleaners.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, he'd done that before the war. He was seen ahead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: First war or second war? Second war.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: First war? No, wait a minute. That was a depression. Depression. The first time he went ahead and he had a shop down there, oh, it was a big place, shelves. But he just had them shelves every place he could pack a second hand vacuum cleaner on them shelves. Spokane still? No, that was in Portland.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But he's got a store in Spokane yet.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, he's still got a store, but his headquarters was in Portland then. But he was seen somewhere there. Well, he was in the vacuum cleaning business before World War I. Before the depression, everything was boom, boom, boom. People were buying cars, buying this, they were trading the vacuum cleaners. Well, he'd take the old vacuum cleaner and sell them a new one. And then if he'd go out and buy some guy up there, a treat, to sell them vacuum cleaners, maybe he'd have a dozen there. He'd go up and make a deal for them and get them. Well, anyway, that's how they'd run me around and get them vacuum cleaners. Well, then the depression hit. And of course, nobody could buy a new vacuum cleaner. So he hired a guy and they went in and he picked up a shop and he'd remodel them, you know, he'd get new brushes and motors, or he'd put the motors on them. And then he'd paint the handles. And he'd put that paper, you know, wrap around the handles. It looked just like a new one, you know. Well, he'd probably get the darn thing for a dollar and a half. During the depression, he'd probably sell it for six dollars. It'd be secondhand, but it looked like a new one, probably. It was awful, but he he was pretty slick, you know, he looked like a brand new machine. And they'd come in and he sold all them off. But nobody else could tell anything. People would buy a vacuum cleaner for him secondhand. Well, that was that was a big go and then he got another wrap at it during World War II. Doing the same thing. But anyway, he branched out, he's got stores in Eugene, Seattle, Tacoma, France Pass.

speaker: Unknown
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When this guy was young, did you have did you have the feeling that he had that kind of go get it?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, he didn't. He didn't. You wouldn't have you know, you just couldn't imagine him going ahead and doing anything like that. You know, he was going to judge all the kids in this neighborhood. Usually, he's the least possible to go get in the place. Well, it turned out just the other way. He's only went to really wet places. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder, you know, there's this they used to talk about rags to riches. You know, you remember that? I read about that that was something it was a big idea. Anybody could get, could become a millionaire. I never have very much believed that myself, because it seems like so terribly few people do get rich.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Do you think

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: do you think that from a case like this, it means that anybody can get rich or you have to be extremely lucky?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, nowadays, of course, them days, you know, there's so few rich people around the United States, there's so few of them really rich people. But nowadays, there's so many people that, you know, they can hand their wealth down to the kids, you know. And they but then this Tark, he was a regular rags to riches. He done it all on his own. He didn't have any in the United States or in nothing. And today, well, he's in that company now, and he was up here a couple, three years ago. I guess a couple years ago he was up here last.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, I think so.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Anyway, we went fishing. Him and I went fishing up at Sand Point. And you know, he's driving a big brand new Cadillac. That didn't make no difference. He'd go along, you know, and old cars would pass him. He had his automatic speed control on it. If he wanted to drive 50, he'd set that. And he'd just set that and steer it and talk, you know. Things would go up hills and down hills. That's all he had to do, just set the steer. And he had another thing that come out on us. It flopped. It was that business of headlights. You know, if you had that magic eye, you know, if you met a car coming in the dim. You'd see them, hadn't you? Well, that had that on it. Well, we were coming down to Sand Point, and that old road you'd be pretty crooked. A lot of curves and up and downs. And that magic eye business. But another thing that happened with that magic eye, well, we went over hills like this, that one place. And there was a car coming. Every time the car would come over a hill, his lights would dim. He was probably a mile away. And then the lights would come on and the lights would go down and come up. But they'd always dim just after that guy. They'd hit him right in the eyes before they'd dim. But they'd dim and then they'd turn black. The danger part was you'd come down here and go around this curve. They had them black markers along the guard rails. And them darn things would dim the lights. Right when you needed lights going around the curve, they'd set them out here in the brush.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty bad idea, right?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, it wasn't a good idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But with this, it seems like you take most of the guys that work in the mill. Yeah. I mean, those guys don't get rich. They could work as hard, harder than anything.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And then by the guys you know, they'd lay you off. There was no time except for like, he was 65, he was pretty tired now. But back there, it was just like a little horse. They'd just turn you out to pasture, you know. You didn't have no pigeon, no nothing, no company. So you said, well, this is it. You don't need to come back anymore. We don't need you. Well, a lot of them didn't have any more than just renting their houses from the potlatch down there. They'd rent their houses and I don't suppose half of them had $50 in the bank or no money laid up. You know, they just couldn't do it. And well, they just went on the county. You just had to go on the county. County aid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that in the old days, oh, in the 20s and in there, when people were working at the mill, you think they thought that they had a pretty good chance of really getting ahead good or were most people just trying to survive?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They just trying to eat three meals a day. They just knew there was, you know, $3.40 an hour for common labor. And like I say, if you had a car, gasoline was around, anywhere from $0.28 to $0.35 a gallon way back in those days. It wasn't cheap. Cheap to tire with gas was $5 for a dollar. That was $0.20 a gallon. That's the cheapest they ever got. $5 for a dollar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems like these days, a lot of people seem to have hope that they're really going to get ahead and really going to get a big stake. Was it the same in those days? Did people think they were going to really get ahead? Well,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: some people can save more than others. You see, it's funny though, when I got to look things over, you take some guy from one or two kids and buy a guy when he gets his retirement age,

speaker: Unknown
words: you want that,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: maybe a guy that had six or seven kids, he'll have a home paid for. It don't make sense. I'm going to tell you he can work the same way just with a guy with six or seven kids, buy a guy when he gets over where he can't work, he's got a home paid for, but the guy's making maybe one or two kids when they get up old, they just don't seem to...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, right now, now, it's awfully hard to feed a family and get ahead. It seems like to me, I hear more young people, you know, complain about you know, it's not payday, you know, I don't know what I'm going to have for supper. I'm just, you know, eat up everything they got and I just don't know what we're...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Of course, people live much different nowadays, you know, people won't do it without a car, they'll have to take meals up, feed it up your table, they still rent a car. Poor people just didn't have cars that day, they'd walk to work, you know, walk back, maybe have an old horse to drive to town if you had to get something heavy to pack on your back while they were driving over Horsetown, poor people just, it wasn't everybody just had cars back in them days. My guys did have cars, like I say, gas leaving was up there at 28, 30 cents a gallon, you know, and you just couldn't afford to drive your car the potluck and back. I walked, even when I had my own car, I walked to work and back, my guys all week lived in the car setting in the shed just to save gas. And a lot of guys up here in Princeton walked to the mill every day, walked back, night and fifth, walked back and forth to work.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Sometimes they caught the train too, it was only charged 10 cents, didn't it, to ride the potlatch?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: 15 cents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not bad.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I'd go over here on a night shift, I'd go over here and catch that, what they call the 3 o'clock train, and go down through Princeton. Well, I'd get on it and ride the potlatch for 15 cents, and then I'd walk clear home that night after work. Well, there was a whole gang that was almost like Cocker's Army coming up the track, you know. It wasn't all alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the guys that lived out in the country and worked in the mill in potlatch felt a little more independent than the men that lived in town? Or wasn't there any difference?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they usually had a little more, they could claim their own. Most of the guys in potlatch, they was trying to live a little bit above their means, I'd say, because they'd try to have better furniture. They was always in debt. Well, just in case, like when the Depression hit, they'd put me, a lot of guys, I was lucky, I got a job night watching. I guess you'd call it lucky, in a way. I had to work 12 hours a night, but then they'd divide it up, I'd work half of the week, and another guy would take the shift to the other half, and divide that work up between us. But I was out there in the yard, and I had that potlatch, and every evening, you could see a car pickup going down the road of the car buyers, new cars, they was hauling them out of town, repossessing them. When they got through hauling cars out, I bet you there wasn't a dozen cars left in the whole town of potlatch. I'm not kidding you. I'm not kidding you. But I didn't go for that new car business that time. Then I got married, she had a little Ford roadster, 27 model, and I had a Ford 27 model coupe. Well, she'd use her car to go schooling back with, and I had that little Ford, so when I got married, we didn't need two cars, we decided a 27 Dodge sedan come up for sale down there, it was a pretty good car, so we decided to trade her two Fords off and get that. So that's what we did. And so when the depression hit, why, the old Dodgers all paid for it. I'll tell you though, I went like them other guys, you know, and they was salesman around trying to sell you new cars before the depression hit, and boy, they was working, they sold a lot of cars. But gee whiz, when they got through hauling cars out of town, I tell you there wasn't any money left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder why they were like that in potlatch. You know, I mean, people couldn't own their homes, right? It was all company. I wonder if that was partly, if you couldn't own your house, you wanted to own something, so you got a car.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, it was a thing to do, you could get a car, and they would clear that little bit of a class distinction, you know, wanted to try to have a little better car than the other guy, you know, bigger, and that was still going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think that doesn't operate so much out in the country here, or out of town.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, if you went out in the country, it didn't seem to make much difference what you drove. I don't know. It didn't seem to make much difference. But in town, that way, I don't know. They said, look across the hall, see the other guy, what he had. Anyway, it caught them all, the depression hit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Quite a few of the guys out here, families here, had a little bit of land too, didn't they? Most of them had a difference.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: A lot of them lived over here in Princeton, you know, and a lot of them were farmers working down there, you know, a lot of my guys, they'd lay off in the harvest, and put their crop in, and they'd go down and work all winter down in the mill, you know. They made pretty good.

speaker: Unknown
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They'd done all right. It was physical the way it was, so there was nothing to look ahead to, you know. You just worked one month the neck of them. If you could keep your nose above water, it worked all right, but when you got old, and they didn't need you, that was it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It really was truly hard. I know one month you was paid $13, your check was, and our gas bill was $12.

speaker: Unknown
words: Depression?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the depression.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: So that wasn't a very good bill, but he had insurance fee, and we dipped into that. That's what put us through the depression.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, a lot of guys, they just got laid off period. Well, they could go down to the store, you know, and hold their hand behind their back and get a check of meat, and they could get their grocery bills, you know. I was out there walking, running around in the snow in the wintertime, making 27 and a half cents an hour, that's what they paid during depression. 27 and a half cents an hour. And, well, they were doing all right, but we had a house right out there, we didn't have too much furniture, and we just got married a year before.

speaker: Unknown
words: Huh?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Couple years.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Really, two years and a half before it really hit us hard.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, by the way, yeah, we got married in 29, I think they shut the mail down about the last part of 31 or 32. Anyway, they had a system there that, they probably was working, now we only had two light bulbs in the house, and we didn't have the electric stove or nothing.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But when I bailed,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: when the electric bill was going up, you know, eight or nine dollars a month, you know, well, they was using me to pay my neighbors' bills, they was pouring on to the guy that was working, to cover the other guys. Well, that was more than I could take, so I asked my dad out of Johnny Hilder's, vacant, so I told our dad, let's just pull out of town. So we moved up there, I don't think that was a mistake. But then, I don't like to have anybody shove a bomb to me like that, you know, but.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it a mistake? Because you had all the things?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, it just worked on a hardship all the way around. I could have just paid down pot that, you know, I guess, and paid that eight, nine, or whatever they wanted to charge me, because I had electricity. But then down to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That wasn't your electricity, though. It wasn't your electricity you were paying.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, we only had to help pay for others. So anyway,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I told the particular house in seventh, and we moved out. Well, we stayed out there, I guess, a couple years.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: We stayed there, we moved up there in November, and then we stayed that winter, and another winter, and then we moved out sometime. I think it was in the fall of the year when we moved back to Potash, wasn't it?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they started to mill up again. Well, how,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what was it like when you were living up there, you weren't working at the mill? Yeah. You were still watchmen?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Still working, yeah. I watched a while, and I worked in the plenary wildly.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Three days a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like that was really going back to the land then.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, yeah, it was. Oh, we, we done all right. But I never did have to take a hand out during that depression. No, I'll say that. But he,

speaker: Anna Utt
words: like I say, he had that insurance, and he cashed it in, you know. And then we were careful. Living up there, no rent, no light snow, water, well, I mean, to pay for. and then, we milked cows, and his folks brought their herd up there, and it fed them out for the winter. And so we had a cow to milk to get our milk, and butter. See, it was a big help that way.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah. But, it was hard. So anyway, we milked tired up in 34. And we moved back to Potlatch. And I stayed there until

speaker: Anna Utt
words: about 43,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I guess, 44 I quit.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, your dad died in February, and we moved up here then to take care of your mother, and that was 43.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I worked a while after that, though.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, you worked until 44.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you lived there, back up on the hill, did you stay at home most of the time? Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, she wasn't in school then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really a whole different kind of a life to make things stretch during the depression? As compared to before?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, well, we truly weren't too bad off, were we, Emmett?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, we wasn't. They just shut the mill down. Kid, Alec McDonald, we worked on the cars together. So anyway, well, we're up against her now, so I had that old Dodge. And we got in it and went over here to Kendrick. And the guy up there raised beans. Well, I got two sacks,

speaker: Unknown
words: a sack of white

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: and big 140 pound bags you know, sacks of beans. How was that? He got two.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: You got two and he got three, wasn't it, Emmett?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I guess I did. Then we were a little deaf in the back of that old Dodge, and hauled them home. You'd get them for about a cent a pound, you know, beans at that time.
