And so by the guys, the whole joke was them beans I hauled had to take care of live that winter. He said, Pop likes to die to death, and had to have his beans. And by the guys, there was more juice than the pies.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And up there, I had a garden, too, that you don't have in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you giving your beans away?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, yeah, we didn't try nothing for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had a garden and grew most all the vegetables?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, after the first winter. And Emmett got a chicken house built, and we had chickens.

speaker: Unknown
words: And we had a chicken house built,

speaker: Anna Utt
words: and it was

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people get together very much during the Depression? No.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, you couldn't. Do what?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Get together in the neighborhood. I mean, neighborhood get-togethers.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Not too much.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Not then.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Everybody stood pretty close to the fire. Oh, in the summertime, they'd eat one half of another out in the Hagen or something like that, you know. Farm workers would help one another out. But I had the other guy didn't have, or my dad had and they didn't have. A lot of swapping done between the farmers. Because nobody had any money.

speaker: Unknown
words: We had

speaker: Anna Utt
words: We worked hard up there. Emmett worked in the mill and then helped with the haying, too, you know, and different things like that. I cooked for the hay crew. So it was It was a busy life, you know. No time to waste. Had orchard up there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: When was it? I bought the Patton place.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, it was after we moved down from the upper place. Yeah. Back to Potlatch.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they had 40 acres up there. They had Earl Patton. And he he had all pretty well cleaned up, you know, and had a nice garden bottom. Fairly good buildings on it. But his dad passed away back in Minnesota. And he had a farm back there and he wanted to get rid of this so he could get money enough to go back there. Well, that guy should have known what he wanted for it. $1,000. Well, that guy should just wasn't easy to find somebody with $1,000 in a day. Finally I talked to the banker and I raised $1,000 and got that 40 acres. So that was my first land purchase. And then I see I was steaming around and got a whole little land one way or another. I got 160 acres way over here on the Sweethearts Biola back in the mountains. Timber claim. I got that just paying back taxes on it. And Of course I didn't get nothing off of that. It's a matter of fact. I gave it to my boy, Frank, before I ever seen the darn place. I never could find it. I don't know. We were looking for it but we just couldn't find the darn place. So that's why I gave it to Frank and then he got a little land with us. I got a friend found it and got it outlined where it was. And he turned around and sold it for I believe $9,000.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I think so, but

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I think I just sold it for him. I got it for paying taxes on it but I didn't get nothing off it otherwise. I gave it to him and he sold it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When I said get together I was thinking of parties and dances too. Because I know I've heard that they used to do that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They did when I was a kid. used to have house parties. They'd have a dance over this place and one here and one week and somebody else would have it the next week during the wintertime especially. that's a thousand times. And at one time I was just a kid over here, this guy over here. He was a big log house there. And then maybe I told you about this. Slim Callahan.

speaker: Unknown
words: Callahan.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Callahan. Slim Callahan. wasn't Callahan. He was a long lady lumberjack you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One of the four L's?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: L's or something like that. He was a good guy but he he was just a lumberjack. He wasn't married to nothing. And those kids was dancing in the house. Those kids was outdoors horsing around. And I wanted to come on him out there and I'm back behind the house down in the weeds laying there mowing and growing. So, well I knew him a little bit because he worked for my granddad Hagan down there. And so I talked to him and he said I wonder who I was? I said, you're a good boy. So finally he said I'm going to give you something. So he pulled out of his pocket and fired out a bill and gave it to me you know. And oh boy I thought I was all of a sudden a millionaire. So my guys, I took it. So I went home that night and the danceman told my dad what happened. That guy gave me, oh I got five dollars. Holy smokes. He worried I shouldn't have taken it you know. So anyway the next day was Sunday and they got me up early and they put me on the Cayuse and I took off and I the lumber camp was right above Harvard someplace. I'd never hardly been up there you know. I'd been up there far beyond Harvard but beyond that I didn't know it. Had a little Cayuse. Name was Dolly. And I fired out that morning I had to go and get that money back to that guy. Like I said, you might as well not come home. That's about what I was mad at you. So I was there all day and way late that evening. But finally I fired him up there in the logging camp. Of course there was other guys around there. So I I told him I was going to give him his five dollars back. He didn't know nothing about it you know. Well I said, did you give it to me last night? My folks said I had to give it back to you. He'd think I was kind of embarrassing around the other guys. You know, you'd throw your money away like that and we were drunk. And anyway, boy I tell you no drunk man ever gave me money after that. That was the end of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you guys were going together though in those days the dances they didn't have them anymore at their homes?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, no they mostly owned the halls. They had a hall up at Harvard and they had pavilions. Well they had one down at Candy Ford down here. A big pavilion down there at the time. They're all gone.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: The dances they just don't have them anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people come from all around? Oh yes. People from the neighborhood?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: All over. Some people go maybe a bunch of people go to Avon up here or to Helmer. They used to have a pavilion up there. A big round hall dance hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard about that one. That was quite a big place.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, it was a big place. And then they had one down at Candy Ford down here. A round hall there. They had one over at Troy. It was on the road going out towards Moscow. Off on the left they were going out towards Moscow. They had a big round hall there. And this place down there, this side of Colfax they called Parvins. I never was down there but they had a big hall there. A lot of people just go to this one. The next day they go to the next one. That's the way they did it. Every Saturday night they'd probably go to a different hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know I've heard a lot about during prohibition. They used to drink and had quite a few fights outside of those dances. It wasn't unusual anyway. Did you get very much lectures from home about that kind of thing? Did they try to make the young ladies have the fear of the alcohol, liquor and that kind of thing? Did parents try to be very strict about that? No.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: We never... Well, I never had a drink and a dance in my life.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know. I never did either.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I don't even think the girls...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Younger people didn't go for it. Most of the older people go to dances that raise all the heck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You as a young sport, you never had drinks at the dances either?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Maybe just a little sip. Just a social sip or something. I never got drunk.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: One time I know he took a sip. Somebody invited him out to the car and reached down and pulled out a bottle. But you never got drunk or anything. Oh no.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I never was that crazy about the stuff.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But... It seemed like if they drank, they'd fight. There's always people that go to the dances to fight.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Get a bottle of Carkatoo and then they have to fight.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But anyway... But it doesn't seem as though we went to all that many dances. For a few years we did and then... What's the matter? Did we grow up?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know. One time I was up at Avon up there at Helmer. I was up there one night. But you wasn't with me then. That's when my cousins come down and those kids all went up there that night. That's when you was there.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I don't remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it all ages of people at those dances?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: All ages. Little kids dances and old women around... I didn't get old fast enough. I mean, I missed out on that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, it just don't happen no more. They don't dance around the country no more. Unless the school kids have a dance in the gymnasium down here. They don't know. Just the school kids. As far as a dance hall, there is one in the old country. I know it. It's just the thing of the past. Television, I guess, is a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Took its place.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Took its place. I haven't even got picture shows. I haven't got a show building in the blues, potlatch. You've got to go to Moscow or Lewiston's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of those picture shows in the 20s? Did kids think very much of them? Were they really great stuff or did you laugh at them or what?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, you know, kind of a funny thing. You take some of them old flop stories you run on television. You probably wouldn't even turn the television on to watch them. But them days, they put a show like that on and put it up in lights and advertisers and people would come all over to watch them. Darn shows, you know, there was metal to them, you know. But the same thing on television and bait. People would give it a time of day to turn the thing on for it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then there was those Wild Wests, you know, and they were really... Well, an awful crowd would go there on the west end.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, Wild Bill Hukakian. And Wild Bill... Bill Hart, all those. Oh, what was that? One of the first cowboys on the theater. Bill Hart. Bill Hart, he's one of the first ones. Oh, one of his shows come on. But the thing is, is that people buy others. Oh, they come out to Pocan with a big show like... Oh, that old Civil War show. You know, I can't think of names. It was a hoop a day.

speaker: Unknown
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: O'Hara... Oh, is it Gone with the Wind?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Gone with the Wind, that's the one. Well, I come out... Well, when I come in to Pocan, the whole neighborhood just practically move up to Pocan that night to see that show, you know. Go clear to Spokane, see Kukai Joe. Or Irv Moskal or Lewiston, wherever they happen to be. Well, they had a cycle like that for a while. People didn't go for these little ding-dong shows they had around here too much. Just maybe people, young people, just go because there's nothing else to do. But anyway, I tell you, take some of the new show, come out. Cripes and neighborhoods just moved to Spokane or Lewiston, wherever the show was. And they'd run that thing there in Pocan maybe for three weeks, you know, before the play out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I heard one, somebody told me that there were some local girls would think they want to go to Hollywood and become movie stars. That was one thing the movies did when they first came.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah, things as well. That was a big thing to get into the movies.

speaker: Unknown
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, I was thinking, Anna, when you're talking about working in Potlatch, it seems really, really tough. I mean, it's too bad now. If all you were getting was your room and board, you know, for doing all that work, I mean, you must have worked hours every day for that.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It started early and some nights it would go late. And in the meantime, you had to get the kids to bed and tell the little guys, see, that was nice. Stories, you know, to make them sleep. I had one girl, I imagine she was five. And you tell her her story and she promised to lay down and go to sleep and all that kind of stuff. And you get through telling her the story. You had to get through pretty soon, you know, because you had work downstairs to do. And she would start screaming. And she would cry and cry and cry. And finally one of the neighbors told the woman I was staying with that I must be beating that child to make her cry that way every night. And so she jumped onto me and I told her what the little kid would do, you know. And you know, she wouldn't believe that. But then the next time I went up there and she was a screamer, I spat at her a little, you know, sit down real hard, you know. And I flopped her down on the bed and then I turned off the light and that was a no-no. And I left her in the dark a little while. But I went up and changed it pretty quick because I thought maybe your mother would come home and not see a light in that room and she'd... So I didn't... But then there were some ladies, we just went through the year doing fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't there... There wasn't any other way that a girl could live in Potlatch without having to... You know, I mean, you just about had to work out if you were going to live there. Mm-hmm.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And putting everyone on the mobile and all those around had their girls.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, that was the thing, you know, high society to have your girls, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the... Did boys from Harvard want... Also live in Potlatch to go to high school or...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, my brother lived out on a farm just outside of Potlatch.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He'd done jewelry for a little bit.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: He went to school three years and... But then I don't know why, whether that farmer died or moved away or what. But then that was as much schooling as he got. He didn't go back the fourth year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He did chores. Was that real hard on him or was that not as bad as...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, it depends on who you are but it's really hard.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I didn't think it was so bad. You see, he didn't have any little kids to deal with. And he would just do his chores, pick apples or whatever had to be done and... And he liked it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any other girls friends that you knew who were working in Potlatch too like you were?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Uh huh. Yeah, the girls were... got so... They treated me like a town girl. And I really didn't mind high school if I'd had time enough to study with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, no time.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And I didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, were there other working girls that you knew who were...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of them country girls too or were some of them town girls who were working? Fine. Were some of those other girls that were working on Nob Hill, were they town girls or were they country girls too?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: All country girls.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: All country girls.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Girls lived in towns. They had their own homes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. Yeah. Did you get very lonely and homesick being in there?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, at first I did. But then I got over it I guess. I must have. Because in my third year, some awful things happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At your Harvard home?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, at home. Family at home got sick. And my dad come down and asked me to come home and help. Well, if I did that, then I'd have to... that would be the end of my schooling. And so I just told him that I'd sure like to finish school. I just didn't want to go home and that'd be the end of it. But you know, I don't know if I'm over that yet or not. I regret it. Because...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They all lived.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Mommas, yeah. They were all real sick. But I... they've been sick so many, many times. Flu's and pneumonia's and different things, you know. We had to pull them through. And John had... what's that one? Disease? Fever?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Rheumatic? Huh? Rheumatic fever?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, no. You'd get out of water. See him. Anyway, he had that. He was sick months and months of that. He got it at Aunt Maud, you know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh. Typhoid?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Typhoid. I couldn't think of that word. Mm-hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father let you stay then because you asked?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah. And he understood it, you know. And then he... I went ahead and finished in school. And then I went down to Lewiston. He helped me through as much as he could. And he was... it was good, hard and awful. The one time I rode up to him for some money. And he didn't have that much money. So he wrote me the nicest letter, telling me I'll send him what I can. And tomorrow I'll send the rest. That was when I was in training. And the girls each had to pay something toward their board in training. So I was no easy.

speaker: Unknown
words: Yeah.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Then when I started teaching, I started giving them back the money that they...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: One funny thing, you know. She was boarding down there when she was going to college, normal. And a bunch of girls lived in a great big two-story house. Each one, some couple or three had a room here and a room there, you know. I don't know what the old house was. It had a lot of rooms, I guess. But anyway, the snow was so deep. Right down in Lewiston, up on Normal Hill, there must have been a good foot of snow in the ground. And that's when they wanted me to come down and take them up to a dance. So I found a place where they lived, and I'd never been there before. But I went in, and there was nobody in the bottom, kind of a wavy room there, you know. And so I was standing around there waiting, just a young boy who I could inquire. Pretty quick, a younger girl, I guess one of the other girl's sisters, younger sisters, she come a galloping down the stairs, and she's standing there, and she stopped and looked at me. I said, Alan, can Anna Grieveson live here? Oh, yes, follow me. Well, she took that back up the stairs again. Well, I guess that was no-man's land up there, you know. But anyway, she said, follow me.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: You did.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And so she come up there, and she went back to the door there. She said, there. So she's rubbed the door wide open. Her and her girlfriend was in the room. They wasn't expecting company. But anyway, I went back downtown. The old car didn't have no heaters in them, you know. So the girl was up in cold night, and the wind blowing blizzard. So I went down and got some of them heavy men's socks, you know, the big wool socks. I got a couple of pair of them for the girls wearing their feet going home. But anyway, I went down and got them and come back. And of course, I went right back up there. I'd been up there once, but I didn't get killed, so I went back up. And all them other doors along the hallway there, you know, you just pull the girls. You can see the doors crack that much and bunch of eyeballs up and down there.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: So we pulled those socks on and left. And that girl kept those socks.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah. Didn't give them back to me. She didn't get me, but she got my socks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty nice living in Lewiston? Did you enjoy that?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, I thought it was. We just had one room, and the bed was in that room, and quite a big bed, you know. And there wasn't much room and a table, a little round table. The stove was out in the hall. It wasn't convenient. But I think we got it for $15 in it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: A room, huh?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I paid seven and a half, and she paid seven.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I imagine about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the girls living there going to Lewiston normal?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they were all true kids, yeah.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Two or three rooms, there was young girls that were going to high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the house have some kind of a matron or somebody living in there to look after everybody? Yes.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they had the landlord.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And if we had a boyfriend come to meet us, or, you know, he was supposed to stay down into the living room downstairs. It was fixed up for the men. But that girl didn't tell him that. She said, well, she's a good girl.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: She comes down there, and she wasn't expecting anybody. She just come and galloping down the stairs, and all, and she sees me standing there, and she looked, you know. I said, well, I think we can live here. Oh, yes, yes, come on. Up the stairs she went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you embarrassed by that, Anna, or did you?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I didn't know how to get him out of there right quick, before the landlady caught us.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Anyway, she doesn't want to go up and knock on the door, you know. She's just about there, and shut the door, and there she is. That's good, though, if my mouth opened. So anyway, finally when I come for her, I said, well, I'll go back down there and get some wool socks for you girls to wear home. So I went on out, and boy, every one of them daughters, I bought them down, girls in all them rooms.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But nobody told on us, I guess. I never got in any trouble.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I never got in trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, when you talked about during the Depression here, and it being pretty hard on, hard, but getting through, how was it with other, the other families living around neighbors? Did they have it as hard or harder, or not so hard as you guys did?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Everybody was short of money. Maybe one or two guys around the country here, bigger farmers might have had a little money, but then it was ordinary. Everybody was just hard up for money by a guess. Everybody had a charge account to store it, but that's what they lived on, and lived on that charge account in Hope. They didn't have money to pay it off. But that kind of finally wound up, and things got to going again. While we were in a mess, now so many people are out of work if it wasn't for handouts. Of course, we didn't get them handouts some days like we do now, you know. You didn't have unemployment insurance, and food stamps, and things they got nowadays. You know, they got it, people think they're bad off, but they're really not bad off compared to what it was during the old, 29 Depression. They don't know what Depression is. People nowadays, they have to pinch in and change their ways a little bit. People actually buy guys who are hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People around here?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I only know one girl, I guess she's just most likely dead. She wasn't much of a neighbor girl up here. She married a guy, and he got a cabin way back up in the woods someplace. That's where they were living. Well then he'd leave her there and take off, you know, and go to Moscow, and horse around. And bad guys, I guess she just stopped and tried to be dead. She wouldn't go home, her parents lived up here, but her parents wasn't agreeable to marry that guy in the first place. Without a pride, she wouldn't go home. So I guess the time they found her, I guess she was too far gone to save. She died anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she die? Yeah. From malnutrition probably, huh?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: From malnutrition, I think it was almost, she just didn't have anything to eat.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: There was a lot of deer killed, you know, during that Depression. Some people even went to extremes, you know, to get a lot more than they'd use.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they just, the Game Warden just didn't see nothing. You know, you'd go out and cut it there, but the way it is though, when you turn people loose, I'll give them that privilege. It was nothing to walk up through the woods and just see a dead deer laying. That was the kind quarter taking off, and the rest of them laying there. And even during that time when the meat was hard to come by, you know. So don't make no difference what you do. There's always somebody that'll make a monkey out of it. Always somebody, by and large.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people around here like Roosevelt a lot when he got in? Did he have a lot of support?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh boy. Oh boy. Yeah. Yeah, sure did. He sure did. In the first term and the second term. The third term, I guess, he was in four times, wasn't he?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, but he didn't live long.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, he didn't live long before. I guess he was the third term when we got in World War II. Well, then getting in that war was a little bit mixed. A little bit mixed feeling. But then he was still a big hero to people. He was a big hero to people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard something. Somebody told me that in the 28 election that Laird came down to the mill and got all the guys together at lunchtime. And got up and gave a speech right before the election. And told him that if Al Smith got elected president, the mill was going to shut down and that was going to be the end of it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I don't remember that part, but the bosses were noisy and that around. If Al Smith got elected, no. Yeah, old Hoover was running again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's 28, yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, old Hoover was running again. When the Democrats got in, I... Let me see, who was president before Wilson? Wilson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that's back...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That goes back behind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wilson was in there in 14.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, we are a Democrat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean 1912. Yeah, Wilson was forgot to be president in 1912. Before him it was Taft. Before him it was...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, Coolidge. Coolidge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Coolidge was after Wilson.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Coolidge was after.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: After, yeah. He was a president all during what they call the boom days of the 20s. Yeah. Well, that's what they call the boom days. Everybody was making a little money and spending it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true? Was that true around here? Was it boom days? Was that boom days here?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, I was trying to tell you about pot life down there. People buying cars like crazy, you know. Everybody was driving around in the new cars. Anybody that didn't have a new car was just nobody. And then they wasn't all little cars. They were big cars, you know. The kind of boys they called. And, uh, my gosh, you know, that's where they got caught. Well, then, my gosh, uh... I'm getting ahead of myself. I'm back in the depression again. Was that what we was? During the depression?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were talking about, uh, about, uh, Coolidge in the 20s.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, that was in the 20s. That was the boom days. Yeah. Yeah, that was in the 20s. Then in 28, Coolidge choose not to run. Yeah. So, Hoover then was a guy. Well, everything was still going good that time yet, you know. Everybody was happy. Hoover got in. And, uh, that's when he run against Al Smith.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mm-hmm. Yeah, you were saying, you were starting, you were saying something about how the, how the bosses were against, uh, Smith. Well... Potlatch. I thought that...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: The biggest blackest, the last I remember of was when, uh, Roosevelt run against, uh, Dewey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, against Dewey.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: When he run against Dewey. That was a big thing then. That's, uh, noise around at that time. I know it, uh, if Republicans didn't get in, that mill would shut down, you know. Well, Dewey didn't get in. Old Truman got in. Mm-hmm. And, uh, so, uh, that was the one I remember of. Mm-hmm. But you know what happened? They started up two shifts. They had to run one shift before, after the election was over with. I was done if I didn't belong to the night shift.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm gonna have to get going, you know. I noticed there. Is that right? Is that after 4.30?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, after 4.30 to 5.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Laura gets to Moscow about quarter five and she's gonna wonder what I'm, where I am. Well, she'll know if she knows I'm up here, but I should really get, get going, so... Don't keep her waiting too long. But we're...
